title: Captivate to Teach: an Old Maxim in the Age of UX & Gamification
excerpt: Principles of UX and gamification can be used to create digital learning experiences that captivate, sustain attention and educate.
content_html:
Behind the usability of a product or service lies a disquieting fact: the digital experience has been designed to persuade us — to move us from indifference to adoption and, in many cases, to compulsive use. In this article we will focus on using it for a nobler purpose: creating digital learning experiences that can captivate and educate their users. 

In a previous post we noted that an online course, like any audiovisual product, is far from neutral; its design can win us over completely or provoke such rejection that learning does not occur. Many digital courses have fallen decades behind cinema, advertising or videogames. And although it is long overdue to close this gap, the question is why have we taken so long? Is it possible to steer persuasive technologies toward an educational goal?

Captivate to teach is an old maxim that goes back to Cicero’s rhetoric (46 B.C.). He advised three duties to the speaker: docere, delectare et movere — to teach, to delight and to move. According to Ciceronian oratory, when we present an idea we must not only persuade our audience by the weight of arguments or demonstrations; we must also delight them with vivid imagination and inflame their passions with the power of eloquence. The art of rhetoric, as systematized by Aristotle, Cicero and Pseudo-Longinus, is not so different from what today’s political campaigns, media, mobile apps and influencer marketing use. And although it is striking how the foundations of persuasion remain largely the same twenty centuries later, what Mediterranean philosophers could not have foreseen was its application to designing digital experiences intended to captivate users so systematically yet invisibly.

Every day we visit dozens of websites and use apps for an increasing range of routine activities: listening to a favorite song, exercising, learning a new recipe, studying or working online. We easily forget that each of these experiences has been designed. In fact, we judge a tool’s usability by its transparency. The tool is there as an extension of our will; we only notice it when our internet connection fails or our mobile device battery dies. But behind a product’s usability lies a disturbing truth: the digital experience has been designed to persuade us — to move us from indifference to adoption and often to compulsive use.

Persuasive technologies influence human behavior using reinforcement pathways similar to those B.F. Skinner employed with laboratory rats in the 1930s.


The functionality, aesthetics and micro-rewards of each interaction operate an invisible but constant rhetoric and, before we realize it, the product or service becomes part of our lives. The fundamentals of persuasion are used daily by the decade’s most popular applications: Facebook, Instagram, YouTube and Twitter. Videogames, for their part, engage ever more consumers with rhetoric of adventure and mastery; their market already surpasses music and film combined and was projected to reach 200 billion dollars per year by 2023. Like any tool, persuasive technology design has a dystopian flip side. Its use can quickly become abuse with harmful consequences for users’ mental and social health. However, in this article we focus on using these techniques for a nobler purpose: applying user experience and gamification techniques to digital products that can fulfill the Ciceronian maxim: teach, delight and move.


USER EXPERIENCE (UX)

A digital learning experience does not have to be the same as an online purchase or installing a new app on our phone. We must balance ease of use with the inherent challenge of the learning journey the user undertakes.

User experience, known as UX by its English acronym, is an interdisciplinary field that studies how users interact with a product or service. It seeks to understand users’ behavior, emotions and underlying attitudes to design experiences that are more intuitive, effective or captivating, increasing the likelihood of adoption. The growing trend toward digitalization and automation of services, widespread mobile device use and the population’s level of digital literacy are among the factors behind the UX boom. Yet the nascent discipline roots itself in human–computer interaction (HCI) and in applying behavioral psychology to technological design. Below we review what UX offers to design more effective and satisfying learning experiences.

1. THE USER IS SELF-INTERESTED: The user cares about themselves and looks for solutions to their needs. Their attention is directed to their life, desires and aspirations. The user is stingy with their time; they will not adopt a product so much as the idea of a product: “this blouse makes me look thinner”; “this fruit juice detoxes me”; “this app gets me to my destination faster.” A UX is relevant when, as designers, we put ourselves in the user’s shoes. If an experience helps the user reach their goals, it is relevant; otherwise it is a waste of time. How does the learning experience I’m designing respond to the user’s desires and aspirations? What can our user accomplish with this new knowledge?

2. THE USER IS ON A JOURNEY: When we meet the user, our experience will be only a small fragment of their journey. Every day we are bombarded with countless stimuli demanding our attention. The user can join our experience or ignore it completely. Every digital experience contains visual or aesthetic cues that serve as a promise, predisposing the user for or against us. What elements of the experience might trigger user rejection or dropout? What elements predispose them positively toward the learning experience?

3. THE USER IS LAZY: In UX terms, taking the path of least resistance is a virtue. As users we are lazy. We want to achieve our objective as simply and directly as possible, without effort or annoying interruptions. That is why UX designers typically shape decision processes so that navigating their interfaces feels like driving on a high-speed highway in a car that practically drives itself. Consider the “I accept the terms” button versus the button to “download the terms” when installing an app. According to a Deloitte study (2017), 97% of people aged 18 to 34 do not read that agreement. But a digital learning experience does not have to be the same as an online purchase or a mobile app installation. We must balance ease of use with the challenge inherent to the user’s learning journey. Which peripheral elements of the learning objective can I modify to make the UX smoother? At what critical moments for learning should I challenge the user with a cognitive break in their experience?

Example of a UX that simplifies navigation through a menu with many possible combinations, bringing the user closer to more immediate satisfaction of their desires. 


4. MENTAL MODELS: Users enter a digital experience with expectations about how to navigate and interact with our graphical interface. These expectations are based on mental models formed from prior experience. Websites we’ve visited, social networks we use daily and videos we’ve watched on YouTube shape our expectations when using a digital solution. A successful UX turns the unknown into the known; its language is intuitive and experiential. Facing a new interface, we quickly recognize where to click, scroll, swipe or hover. An interface that is easy to use is typically one that is easy to recognize. What language does our learning solution speak? What familiar features can we incorporate to enrich our content?

5. CENTRAL & PERIPHERAL INFLUENCE: When we are motivated to learn about a topic, we pay little attention to superficial aspects of the experience. The information we care about might be on a barely legible photocopy, or the foremost authority on the subject might give a lecture in pajamas. This is the central route of influence; it appeals to the educational value of the information. But the user may be unfamiliar with the topic or have never heard of the presenter. If the central route fails, the user will turn to superficial cues and judge based on aesthetic or emotional considerations. Both routes are used by digital experiences and are rarely found in a pure state. How familiar is our audience with the topic or its presenters? What peripheral cues can we use to make our content more persuasive?


GAMIFICATION

When we are motivated by personal fulfillment, we lose track of time, forget our surroundings and commit our whole being to the task. Self-actualization is not a prize or a temporary satisfaction, but the meaning that affirms the entirety of the path traveled.

Gamification is the art of applying the elements that make a game stimulating and addictive to everyday, work or educational contexts. Although gamification is controversial due to its capacity to act directly on dopamine levels in the brain, its use in learning contexts can have positive effects on concentration, alertness and user motivation. Through videogame mechanics, dynamics and aesthetics, we can engage users with the pedagogical goal of our digital adventure. Below we examine the main game elements that can serve as sources of motivation.

The short film Sight (2012) depicts a future of radical gamification operating via an augmented reality (AR) implant in the user’s retina. 


1. ADVENTURE: An adventure is not a mere episode of life but an extraordinary experience that interrupts the ordinary flow of events. The fascination of an adventure is that it removes the conditions and obligations of daily life and propels us into the unknown, where difficulties await but also extraordinary rewards. A game calls us to adventure with a meaningful story in which we can take part. It makes us lose track of time and surrender fully to the activity. Story is the central axis around which all other game features revolve. The story need not be realistic; it can take place here and now, on another planet, or in the future. What matters, as Aristotle’s Poetics recommends, is that it be plausible. For designers of learning adventures, this means the player must temporarily suspend disbelief and fully immerse in the narrative. How can we turn our instructional content into a meaningful adventure the user wants to dive into?

2. MISSION: All heroic quests, from Homer onward, have a mission, which may be stated at the adventure’s outset or remain hidden like the meaning of one’s life. Ithaca, the golden fleece, the promised land, or nirvana are all variations of a mission that justifies the adventure and gives transcendent meaning to the hero’s actions. The mission not only organizes the player’s actions into a coherent whole but becomes a challenge demanding focus and concentration, contributing to an optimal mental state for learning. What explicit or implicit mission have we presented to our player? What transcendent meaning does our mission give to the player’s actions?

3. DISCOVERY: A digital adventure invites discovery. Discovery is a form of exploratory play in which we uncover the space of possibilities within the game. Our scientific spirit encourages us to explore the sensory environment, run small experiments to observe the effects of our actions, or infer the rules and exceptions of this miniature world. This exploratory phase is necessary to become familiar with the game universe and can be very useful for promoting generative learning. When an educational adventure is based on a scientific model of a well-described phenomenon, such as natural selection or supply-and-demand economics, we can give users control over the variables that govern the universe, as MinuteLabs.io simulators do. What features of my digital adventure invite players to active discovery? How can I turn exploration and experimentation into learning opportunities?

Interactive version of Primer Learning’s video "Simulating Natural Selection." Created by MinuteLabs


4. RULES: Within a game, the ambiguous laws of ordinary life are temporarily replaced by precise, arbitrary rules freely accepted by the player when the adventure begins. Rules constrain the game within limits that must not be broken under threat of penalties or infractions. A player will discover the rules of our created universe by seeking shortcuts to reach the goal or obtain a reward. In a learning context, rules postpone immediate gratification, encourage observation, analysis and strategic thinking. What are the rules of our game? How do we use rules to educate and stimulate the player?

5. RISKS: Although a digital game can present real risks to users’ physical or mental integrity — addiction, bankruptcy or even death — here we refer to risks within the game world to the player’s character or avatar. There is no adventure without risk. Typically a game character has a limited number of lives or a finite energy supply. Within the game we encounter numerous threats to the character’s integrity, from hordes of zombies to a mineral from the planet Krypton. Risks, like game rules, set boundaries and force the player to plan moves, conserve scarce resources and find creative ways to avoid threats. Every adventure involves something to lose. It speaks directly to our instinct for preservation — the same drive that has led to humanity’s greatest inventions. What risks can we incorporate into our learning adventure to stimulate player creativity?

6. REWARDS: Rewards in a digital adventure can be coins, gems, extra lives or reflect the player’s new status. Rewards are a valuable opportunity to add layers of satisfaction to the game experience. A reward is satisfying when it combines visual and auditory effects that please the senses. This kind of reward produces a powerful reinforcing effect and can be useful in learning contexts to build habits or develop skills that require sustained practice. However, if rewards depend solely on player skill they become too predictable and their effect wanes. One reason gambling is so seductive is precisely the size of rewards and their unpredictability. Adding an element of surprise can increase players’ alertness. How am I rewarding the player in my learning adventure? What surprise effects can I include to keep alertness high?

7. SOCIAL ELEMENT: In social species like ours, play is an opportunity to compete, collaborate, predict a co-player’s actions or simply share an inherently pleasurable activity with others. Videogames that allow two or more simultaneous players leverage social psychology. Players compete for rewards or for who reaches the goal first. They seek to influence others’ decisions, becoming allies, opponents, leaders or specialists. Co-players’ actions can also be instructive and model behavior for one or more players, fostering social learning. What elements of competition, collaboration or social learning opportunities can we include in our digital adventure? How do we integrate our learning solution with social networks?

Approaching users’ psychology and sources of motivation will allow us to create learning solutions that meet both their goals and ours simultaneously.

8. SELF-ACTUALIZATION: Finally, as a miniature universe, the culmination of a game is always the player’s realization. The hero’s apotheosis occurs when their labors end and they reap the fruit of their efforts. For Hercules it meant becoming a god; for the Buddha, achieving enlightenment and the liberation of all beings. For most of us, self-actualization means actualizing our potential and becoming the best version of ourselves. Rules, risks and rewards appeal to our preservation instinct; competition to our thirst for social validation. In those cases we are driven by desire for something we lack and its eventual satisfaction. But self-actualization grants the player access to a space of personal transcendence, making it the most inexhaustible source of motivation. When motivated by self-realization we lose track of time, forget our surroundings and commit fully to the task. Self-actualization is not a prize or temporary gratification but the meaning that affirms the entire journey. Of all motivation sources, this may be the hardest to incorporate into a digital learning adventure because it requires content suited to speak the language of transcendence. Is it possible to give our content a transcendent meaning?

﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿The documentary Mountain (2017) shows how mountains bring humans closer to a realm of transcendence that can justify risking one’s life. ﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿

In this article we have reviewed what persuasive technologies can offer to create more effective, captivating and meaningful digital learning experiences. Some instructional content is better suited to being fully gamified, while for others it will suffice that the user achieves a learning objective without technology becoming an obstacle. The important thing is to know the available tools and the influence they can have on user behavior. Approaching their psychology and sources of motivation will allow us to create learning solutions that meet both their goals and ours simultaneously.


REFERENCES

Caillois, Roger. (1958) Man, Play and Games. Trans. Meyer Barash. University of Illinois Press.

Cicero, Marcus Tullius (46 B.C.) Orator or Accomplished Speaker. Trans. Edward Jones. E-book.

Clark, A. (2004). Natural-born Cyborgs. Minds, Technologies, and the Future of Human Intelligence. Oxford University Press.

Gadamer, Hans Georg (1960) Truth and Method. Trans. Joel Weinsheimer & Donald G. Marshall. Bloomsbury Revelations.

Goethe, Ole (2019) Gamification Mindset. Springer International Publishing. Human-Computer Interaction Series.

Huizinga, Johan (1938). Homo Ludens: A Study of the Play-Element in Culture. Boston, MA. Beacon Press.

Kim, Sangkyun et al (2018) Gamification in Learning and Education: Enjoy Learning Like Gaming. Springer International Publishing.

Oliveira, Wilk & Bittencourt Ig Ibert (2019) Tailored Gamification to Educational Technologies. Springer International Publishing.

Stull, Edward (2018) UX Fundamentals for Non-UX Professionals: User Experience Principles for Managers, Writers, Designers, and Developers. Apress.
source_title: Cautivar para enseñar: una vieja máxima en tiempos de UX & Gamification
source_excerpt: Los principios de UX y gamificación pueden utilizarse para crear experiencias de aprendizaje digital capaces de cautivar, sostener la atención y educar.

title: Interactivity
excerpt: Interactivity transforms the presentation of information into an active experience that promotes more effective, meaningful, and lasting learning.
content_html:
More than seventy experimental studies have shown that a robust interactive design produces more effective and lasting learning.


In the previous post on instructional design, we proposed a generative view of learning in which users play an active role in creating the knowledge to be taught. From this perspective, effective instructional design should not be limited to presenting new information, but should stimulate learners’ active discovery process that characterizes authentic learning. Certainly, the way we present content should support retention, organization, and integration of new information, increasing the likelihood that the learner will use it in the future. But how does our generative learning approach reflect in the architecture of our digital solution? This is a question we can only answer by observing the level of interactivity of our content. Interactivity lies at the heart of most of our authentic learning experiences. Our content can faithfully follow other principles of effective instructional design — be multimedia, coherent, simple, and emotionally engaging — but if the ability to interact with the content is limited by our solution’s architecture, we will hardly be facilitating authentic learning. To assess the interactivity level of our content we must ask: What is the user's real participation in the creation of the knowledge we deliver? Is it limited to clicking buttons to play or pause videos? Do they answer knowledge checks after each content block? Are they asked to apply what they learned in formative activities? Do they receive feedback on their performance? Can they try again incorporating the feedback received? More than seventy experimental studies (Bernard et al., 2009) have shown that asynchronous digital courses offering users a robust interactive design achieve better learning outcomes in impact evaluations.


Although the principle of interactivity is intuitive and practically indisputable, it has long been sidelined in formal disciplines, which are usually taught in a structured, linear fashion, focusing on the logical presentation of content rather than on the learner. Under that approach the learner is a passive recipient of information who, at best, can retain key elements and successfully pass a knowledge test. By contrast, when we learn a motor skill—such as riding a bicycle, dancing, or drawing in perspective—we can immediately observe the results of our performance. By comparing our performance to that of an expert, we can correct the attributes required to achieve the desired result. If the instructor also helps identify which performance attributes need correction and trains specific skills aimed at a measurable outcome, our progress will be even greater. In all these cases, learning starts from the learner’s own actions. We therefore call this interactive learning: content produces an effect on the learner, who in turn acts on the content, exploring it in multiple directions; selecting elements that attract attention; reorganizing the presented information; or testing their knowledge. All these learner-driven actions allow them to make sense of the content, turning it into something that belongs to them and sometimes accompanies them for life.

An interactive design is characterized by incorporating interactions or scripts of the form “If the user does A, then B.” Interactive learning occurs starting from the learner’s own actions.

Decades of research in primary and higher education support the idea that reducing “teaching” hours while providing learners with more and better feedback yields better results. This underscores the importance of feedback when designing interactions in our content. Feedback is the process by which a system gathers information about its actions in the environment and modifies its strategy to self-regulate. All living beings are self-regulating systems. When we walk in the sun on a hot day we begin to sweat; evaporation cools the air in contact with our skin and thus our body lowers its temperature. Our metabolism changes with the season or with blood cortisol levels. Our red blood cell production increases when exposed to high altitude. All of this occurs without conscious awareness; these are innate responses programmed at the genetic level. Over their evolution, species in our biosphere have acquired certain attributes—specializations—that allow them to adapt to their environment. Our most specialized organ is undoubtedly the brain. Its capacity lies in producing non-innate responses such as language, music, numbers, or poetry. Unlike the innate responses, these are learned, but feedback—the ability to learn from the effects of our actions—continues to hold a place of honor in both cultural and biological learning.

In general terms, an interactive design is characterized by incorporating interactions or scripts of the form “If the user does A, then B.” These interactions can range from basic actions, like navigating a content index, to non-linear experiences such as decision trees. As noted, not all digital architectures currently supporting asynchronous content allow a sufficient level of interactivity to guarantee meaningful learning experiences for users. In a future post, when we discuss virtual classrooms (LMS) and asynchronous content authoring tools, we will explore the market alternatives in greater depth. For now we will limit ourselves to discussing what types of interactions we can incorporate into our content using available technology and how they bring us closer to the learning objective.


1. CONTENT INDEXES: Undoubtedly one of the most basic interactions, it is nonetheless a powerful resource to organize and synthesize content; it gives the user a navigation map that supports retention and organization of the presented information. Indexes can consist of text and/or images and be navigated in one or more directions. When the order in which the index is navigated does not alter comprehension, we recommend not forcing a sequence of interactions. After reviewing a section of the index, the text/image element representing it should change to a “viewed” state, providing a visual confirmation of the user's progress.


2. EXPANDABLE/DROPDOWN CONTENT: Another powerful resource is expandable content. Its simplest form is a text label that appears when hovering over or clicking an element in the learning window. The expanded content can be an image, text, audio, or video. The advantage is delivering additional information without visually cluttering the learning window while preserving higher-level relationships among visible elements. A common example is websites where hovering over a product shows a dropdown with a detailed view or technical specifications. Similarly, when presenting the anatomy of an organ like the brain, each part can be turned into an interactive button that, when clicked, shows expandable content with its name, functions, or any material that provides a second level of analysis without losing sight of the whole and the relative position of each part.



3. DRAG-AND-DROP ACTIVITIES: This type of interaction descends from early graphical interfaces that provided a visual representation of documents, folders, directories, or applications that previously required commands to manipulate. In a learning context, the user selects an item in the learning window and drags it to a location, triggering positive or negative feedback displayed in an expandable area. This interaction can be used to associate concepts, illustrate spatial relations, sequences, or diagrams. Drag-and-drop activities support the user's mental representation process by providing visual scaffolding that facilitates concept retention while testing mastery of the delivered information.


4. 360º IMAGE/VIDEO: This technique creates an immersive environment using 360º images or videos. The user can navigate these images from the capture point by dragging the cursor on their computer; using their mobile phone; or via a virtual reality (VR) interface that interprets the user's physical movement to rotate the image or video in the required direction. Alternatively, 360º images and videos can include hotspots or interactive buttons that, when selected, reveal multimedia content or link to one or more 360º images, as seen in museum virtual tours or applications like Google Maps. In a learning context, the main advantage of this technique is the hyperrealistic representation of a space that invites exploration, stimulating an active discovery process in the user.


5. DECISION TREES: Decision trees are sets of scripts of the form “if the user does A, then B; if the user does Y, then Z” that allow various routes or branches of content based on user decisions. Decision trees likely inherit from early interactive fiction experiments (see Borges, 1941) and children’s “Choose Your Own Adventure” books popularized in the mid-1970s. A recent example of interactive cinema is Bandersnatch (2018) from the Black Mirror series. In a digital decision tree, the content flow is interrupted by a question, dilemma, or invitation to interact with one or more visual elements in the learning window. The user's response triggers a sequence or branch of content that shows the effects of their decision. In a learning context, the advantages are numerous. First, a question or dilemma interrupting the expository flow stimulates inference generation from the information provided. The user faces a decision whose consequences they can learn from and revise their strategy for the next opportunity. A decision tree can also provide tangible feedback about the consequences of an incorrect decision in scenarios where the real-world risk is unacceptable, such as piloting an aircraft or operating heavy machinery. Finally, a decision tree forces us to think in a space of interaction possibilities rather than a single interaction route, allowing our content to better adapt to each learner's needs.



Bandersnatch (2018) An interactive film


In this post we reviewed the relevance of the interactivity principle and a series of resources and techniques that can be used independently or combined to enhance generative learning experiences. Today, asynchronous content authoring software offers almost unlimited interactivity possibilities, incorporating complex interactions that distill the full formative potential of real or hypothetical situations. However, we must remember that above all, robust instructional design keeps the learning objective in focus; therefore our recommendation is to use these resources coherently and economically, purging unnecessary interactive paraphernalia from our content whenever possible.


REFERENCES:

Bernard, R et al. (2009) A Meta-Analysis of Three Types of Interaction Treatments in Distance Education. Review of Educational Research 79(2)

Borges, J.L. (1941) Examen de la obra de Herbert Quain. Obras completas Emecé, Grupo Editorial Planeta (2009).

Contreras, J.I. (2019) Playing with pattern: aesthetic communication as distributed cognition. Aisthesis 12(1): 27-39.
source_excerpt: La interactividad transforma la presentación de información en una experiencia activa que favorece un aprendizaje más efectivo, significativo y duradero.

title: What is instructional design
excerpt: Instructional design combines a discipline grounded in the psychology of learning with a process for creating experiences that facilitate the generation of new knowledge.
content_html:
Instructional design is both a discipline and a process aimed at creating learning experiences that facilitate the generation of new knowledge. As a discipline it is historically linked to the psychology of learning, which examines how human beings learn. As a process, it is the primary activity behind the development of a successful learning experience.

Most of our learning experiences are not designed. In early childhood we learn to walk by stumbling; we babble our first words and no one teaches us what inside, outside, on top, underneath, in front or behind mean. We learn all this situated in a life context that is often erratic, without compass or map, yet supported by the reliability of instincts honed over hundreds of generations. The brain is our most specialized organ. Just as felines have claws for hunting prey and plant leaves have chlorophyll for photosynthesis, our brain evolved the ability to acquire and generate information that allows us to adapt to the complex environments we inhabit. Thus we could say that learning is the quintessential human capacity; the one that enables the development of culture and a distinctly human life. From this we can conclude that we are born to learn and yet… why is it so hard to learn certain things? What is required to learn? Which factors facilitate learning and make it memorable? Instructional design aims to answer these questions. Instructional design is both a discipline and a process aimed at creating learning experiences that facilitate the generation of new knowledge. As a discipline it is historically linked to the psychology of learning, which studies how humans learn and therefore its principles have shifted with prevailing paradigms. As a process, it is the first activity behind the development of a successful learning experience.

Instructional design as a discipline

Let us first consider instructional design as a discipline. During the 1950s the earliest models of this emerging field focused on instructional objectives, repetition and feedback. Their goal was to produce a predictable response in the learner through practice and reinforcement. While the principle of reinforcement remains valid for most learning contexts, it is neither the only nor the sufficient guide for our design process. Later models highlighted distinct domains of learning: cognitive —what one understands or thinks—; psychomotor —what one performs or executes—; and affective —what one feels or prefers.

For example, I can learn to identify the parts and functions of a machine, but that does not mean I have learned how to operate it. Once I have learned to operate the machine, that does not mean I will prefer to operate it in a way that reduces accidents or extends its lifespan. Therefore, for the learning goal to be achieved, it must first be broken down into specific objectives for each domain: identify and understand the purpose of each component; operate the components in a specific sequence; and prefer modes of operation that prolong lifespan and prevent accidents. What the learning domains made visible is that mere exposure to a stimulus and reinforcement of the desired response are insufficient for learning to occur. Depending on the learning objective, instructional design must adapt strategies to produce change in each specific domain (cognitive, psychomotor and affective). As we will see later, the learning domains remain fully relevant in the instructional design process.

In the mid-1960s Robert Gagné published his Conditions of Learning and proposed that there are levels or hierarchies of learning, ranging from simple stimulus/response, through association and conceptualization, to the resolution of complex problems. Gagné’s hierarchies emphasize that each level of learning depends on the immediately preceding level, so instructional design should mirror this sequence in a system of stages or prerequisites that bring learners progressively closer to the objective, creating the ideal conditions for higher-level learning. This sequence is commonly called a learning path, and we see examples of it in formal curricula. The assumption is that we learn best when moving from general to specific; from simple to complex; from theory to practice. Undoubtedly, logical sequence is a foundation of explicit knowledge. Without it we could not describe complex processes nor reach what Piaget called formal operations—the ability to manipulate abstract objects independently of the concrete world, which underpins all disciplines of knowledge. However, later models began to ask whether formal learning through acquisition of explicit knowledge is always the most appropriate route to install new capabilities.

From the 1970s onward, Jean Piaget’s ideas inspired Jerome Bruner and other constructivists to a radical paradigm shift: instead of a linear instructional design that guides the learner through a logical sequence culminating in the learning objective, learners began to be conceived as creators of their own learning conditions. Rather than stating principles or axioms and deducing consequences and applications, the instructor should encourage learners to test what they know, formulate their own hypotheses inductively and discover for themselves the principles of the knowledge to be taught. As mentioned earlier, we are born to learn. Through exploration and play we form inferences and mental schemas that we constantly test in childhood. Knowledge of the world does not arise linearly but through a spiral in which we continuously revisit, refine and transform what we have learned to adapt to new evidence or more complex scenarios. This spiral logic is present in the organic growth we call morphogenesis, in language development and in scientific revolutions. Why should learning work differently? One of the most significant contributions of this paradigm change is the application of informal learning principles to instructional design. We learn all the time, often without noticing, and solve problems in complex domains before we can state any principle. An important Dutch study (Borghans et al., 2014) showed that in the corporate context, of an average 505 hours of annual learning per employee, 96% occurs via informal learning and only 21 hours are attributable to formal company-led training. In the past decade asynchronous digital learning technologies (https://www.rasika.cl/post/aprendizaje-a-distancia-una-breve-gu%C3%ADa-anti-naufragios) have enabled the design of non-linear learning experiences—fully flexible and adjustable to learners’ styles and pace. Collaborative knowledge platforms are decentralizing the instructional design process and emphasizing the advantages of tools and systems that let users create their own culture of continuous learning. This paradigm shift, whose full realization we are beginning to experience with new information technologies, leads us to the second aspect: instructional design as a process.

Instructional design as a process

Many digital courses have lagged for decades behind film, advertising and videogames. It is time to close that gap! The same content can feel completely foreign or inspire such identification that learning becomes a natural consequence. Captivate to teach is an old maxim that, in our era of audiovisual over-stimulation, has become imperative.

When we talk about asynchronous digital learning, instructional design is the cornerstone of developing a successful learning experience. After briefly reviewing the paradigm shift this discipline has undergone, let us examine some principles to consider when designing asynchronous digital learning. The following principles have been adapted from the fourth edition of Clark & Mayer (2016) and modified based on professional experience.

1. MULTIMEDIA: When discussing learning domains we emphasized that each domain—cognitive, psychomotor, affective—requires an appropriate instructional design strategy. The instructional designer’s role is not only to deliver information but to facilitate its processing and assimilation. When narration, images and sounds are presented simultaneously and complementarily, we guide the learner in generating new concepts and mental models. Therefore, graphic or audio resources are not mere decoration or seductive adornment in a learning experience; they should enhance content and make it more resonant (https://dle.rae.es/pregnancia). Criteria for determining whether we are using suitable multimedia resources are: a) Transformational: the resource illustrates changes over time or space that add descriptive clarity to the content; or b) Interpretive: the resource makes phenomena or processes that are normally unnoticed in ordinary experience tangible. Below you can review two examples illustrating their use cases.

Transformational graphic

Interpretive graphic


2. COHERENCE: According to the coherence principle, every element used in instructional design must be consistent with the learning objective. Our processing capacity and working memory are limited, so the recommendation is to ensure that all stimuli—text, narration, images and sounds—are essential to make sense of the content. Adding attractive but superfluous elements is likely to interfere with learning by diverting the learner’s attention to irrelevant stimuli that consume limited memory resources, or by disrupting the generation of mental models through decorative or merely seductive graphics. Interest cannot be sprinkled onto content like a condiment; it must be woven into the presentation itself, into its gestalt. Coherence is the harmonious relationship among all elements used to present content, conspiring to produce the discovery that characterizes meaningful, lasting learning.

3. SIMPLICITY & BREVITY: The principle “less is more” has earned an honoured place across many knowledge domains, and learning is no exception. This principle governs several aspects of instructional design simultaneously. The first aspect concerns content brevity. We can usually assimilate only a limited amount of information within a given time. Our attention is quickly diverted by the multitude of information sources we continuously face. For this reason instructional design now favors brief learning capsules rather than long blocks of content. One way to achieve simplicity in extensive content is to divide it into indices or navigation maps, giving users flexibility to assimilate it at their own pace. The second aspect concerns graphic simplicity. Research shows that two-dimensional illustrations with fewer realistic details support retention and conceptualization. Another aspect of simplicity is how we present on-screen text. Journalism and graphic design have long used ledes or summary extracts. In a newspaper we often find highlighted information in a larger font that synthesizes a block of text in a few lines. Besides simplifying, ledes and extracts pique interest and support retention of essential content elements.

4. EMOTIONAL DESIGN: The influence of emotions on how we process information is unavoidable. How we perceive the world and make decisions depends on the physiological reaction certain stimuli—biologically relevant to our survival and reproduction—produce in us. Emotions also impact retention and memory encoding. Recent studies (Tyng et al., 2017) have experimentally shown that “emotionally charged events are remembered more clearly, with greater accuracy and for longer periods than neutral events” and that cortisol released in response to such events may play a modulatory role in learning (McGaugh, 2015).

All of the above underscores the need for emotional design in our content. The importance of this principle merits a dedicated entry in this guide. For now, suffice it to say that instructional content, like any audiovisual product, is not neutral; its emotional design can fully engage us or provoke rejection so strong that learning does not occur. Many digital courses have lagged for decades behind film, advertising and videogames. It is time to close this gap! The same content can feel completely foreign or inspire such identification that learning becomes a natural consequence. Captivate to teach is an old maxim that, in our era of audiovisual over-stimulation, has become imperative.

5. INTERACTIVITY: Interactivity lies at the heart of most authentic learning experiences. When faced with a real problem that requires decision-making or choosing a course of action, we test the mental models we have formed about the surrounding reality. There are two types of interaction in an asynchronous digital learning experience. The first is purely behavioural, where the user clicks a button to proceed, drags one or more objects, or even participates in a virtual reality simulation similar to a videogame. This type of interactivity alone is not sufficient for learning. It requires a second type—psychological interaction—which refers to the degree to which presented information stimulates an active processing rather than a merely receptive process. A lesson may have a high degree of behavioural interactivity, but if the interactions do not guide attention to the relevant aspects, help organize content mentally into a coherent structure, or integrate it with prior learning, learning will not occur. Today’s digital content creation software offers almost limitless interactivity possibilities; however, the challenge for meeting instructional objectives is to turn these interactions into opportunities for generating new knowledge, not into sophisticated features that cancel the active discovery process that characterizes authentic learning.


360° images enable virtual tours of a physical space and the addition of hotspots—buttons that provide extra content or link to photos, video or text.

6. PERSONALIZATION: One of instructional design’s goals is to facilitate the generation of new knowledge, so it is learner-centred. In a face-to-face course a facilitator can use physical presence, tone of voice, nonverbal language and movement to generate interest and maintain attention. If the facilitator is also skilled at remembering names or invites participants to share personal experiences that are then coherently integrated into the presentation, they can produce a powerful social effect that supports the learning objective. The presence of another human has a magnetic effect on attention and perception. We allocate more cognitive resources to understanding a friend’s testimony than to listening to a news bulletin, or the voice of a robot informing us of an overdue payment. Similarly, asynchronous digital content benefits from a conversational style that uses first and second person rather than the formal third-person tone of announcements and operation manuals. A warm voice in course narration and friendly feedback that uses our name can significantly increase engagement with the content, reinforcing the emotional design principle.


Starbucks was a pioneer in personalized marketing


Evidence suggests that even when we know there is no flesh-and-blood person behind the screen, part of our brain is convinced otherwise and allocates more and better resources to enhance the learning experience in response to these social cues. The challenge for personalized instructional design is to be flexible enough to adapt to the learner’s needs without losing control of the instructional objective.

In this entry we have addressed instructional design as both discipline and process. While all the principles presented are relevant and worth considering for practice aimed at the best results, the emphasis we give each principle in any particular design should be guided by the learning objective, learners’ prior knowledge, and the cultural and technological context in which we deploy our learning solution.


REFERENCES

Borghans, L., D. Fouarge, A. de Grip & J. van Thor (2014). Werken en leren in Nederland (report). Researchcentrum voor Onderwijs en Arbeidsmarkt (ROA), Maastricht University.

Clark, Ruth & Mayer, Richard (2016) E-learning and the science of instruction: proven guidelines for consumers and designers of multimedia learning. Wiley. New Jersey, USA.

McGaugh, James. (2015) ‘Consolidating memories’. Annual Review of Psychology Vol. 66:1-24

Tyng, Chai M. et al. (2017) ‘The Influences of Emotion on Learning and Memory’. Frontiers in Psychology, 8:1454: 17.
source_excerpt: El diseño instruccional combina una disciplina basada en la psicología del aprendizaje con un proceso para crear experiencias que facilitan la generación de nuevos conocimientos.

title: Maintaining Academic Integrity in the Age of AI: Institutional Priorities
excerpt: The challenge is not just detecting fraud, but designing AI tutors that recognize the evaluative context, protect formative challenges, and reinforce learning where performance is low.
content_html:
The use of AI in higher education is no longer marginal. The Observatorio article cites Ruskulis et al. (2026): in a survey of 248 students, 76% reported using AI regularly for academic tasks and 65% preferred ChatGPT. Students themselves associated excessive reliance with a breach of academic integrity. The challenge is not only to detect fraud, but to establish clear policies, AI literacy, and assessments that preserve critical thinking, authorship, and authentic learning.

Evidence

The study combined a student survey with faculty interviews to examine how AI is used and what tensions it creates in academic writing. Its findings point to an important paradox: students recognize the ethical risk of over-relying on these tools and, at the same time, routinely incorporate them into their tasks. This demands transparent rules and assessment experiences that keep cognitive effort active.

Why it matters

An AI tutor can support learning or, if it ignores the evaluative context, become a way to complete quizzes, interactive activities, or tests without demonstrating mastery. Integrity must be part of the assistant’s architecture: which events it listens to, when it limits help, what it logs, and how it returns the student to legitimate formative practice.

Rasika’s perspective

In a CourseMentor integration with an LMS, SCORM, or xAPI, the assistant listens to progress events and assessment state. When it detects that a student is completing a quiz, a drag-and-drop, or another graded activity, it activates an integrity mode: it does not provide answers or hints that would allow bypassing the challenge and can temporarily restrict response generation. Outside that context, the same telemetry can detect low performance, recommend course-specific content, and generate practice interactions to verify mastery before proceeding. These rules are configured with the institution and include escalation criteria and human review.

Related service

  • CourseMentor SaaS (AI Assistant): contextual protection during assessments and personalized reinforcement based on course progress events.

Learn about CourseMentor and its LMS integration

Editorial source: How to Ensure Academic Integrity in the Age of AI?

Cited study: Ruskulis et al. (2026), An Academic Text: The Balance Between Academic Integrity and Artificial Intelligence
source_title: Mantener la integridad académica en la era de la IA: prioridades institucionales
source_excerpt: El reto no es solo detectar fraude, sino diseñar tutores IA que reconozcan el contexto evaluativo, protejan los desafíos formativos y refuercen el aprendizaje donde existe bajo desempeño.

title: Completing courses is not the same as adoption: what to measure to know if it works
excerpt: Completing a course does not prove adoption. To determine whether training is effective, measure sustained use, on-the-job behavior changes, and reductions in support requests.
content_html:
The text emphasizes that completing a course does not equate to real adoption. It recommends measuring indicators that reflect sustained use (beyond the first two weeks), on-the-job behavior, and a decrease in support calls. It also notes the need to instrument adoption at the team level without turning it into surveillance.

Why it matters

For L&D and technology leaders, this redefines KPIs: moving from completion rates to metrics that measure transfer and sustainability (retention of use, ticket reduction, observable task changes). It requires designing instrumentation and privacy and governance policies.

Rasika's perspective

CourseMentor connects to the client's LMS and can listen to what happens during course delivery, generating previously invisible metrics such as dropout rate, learning activities with lower success rates, or the users' frequently asked questions that reach the chatbot. These metrics allow design teams to focus their efforts and test new strategies.

Original source: Completion Is Not Adoption: What To Measure When The Goal Is Workers Actually Using The Tool
source_title: Completar cursos no es lo mismo que adoptar herramientas: qué medir para saber si funciona
source_excerpt: Completar un curso no demuestra adopción. Para saber si la formación funciona, conviene medir el uso sostenido, los cambios de comportamiento en el trabajo y la reducción de solicitudes de soporte.
